g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of cache memory architecture, a fundamental concept in modern computing systems.
Cache memory bridges the speed gap between fast processors and slower main memory, dramatically improving system performance.
g.text: Processors are much faster than memory. Cache acts as a high-speed buffer storing frequently accessed data.
This bridging technique solves the memory wall problem without expensive memory upgrades.

g.data-narration: The memory hierarchy arranges different memory types by speed and size, with fastest at top and slowest at bottom.
Three primary cache mapping techniques determine how main memory blocks are placed in cache lines.
g.text: Main memory blocks can be mapped to cache lines using three different approaches:

g.data-narration: In direct-mapped cache, each memory block has only ONE possible cache line location determined by modulo operation.
g.text: Each memory block maps to exactly ONE cache line using:
Cache Line = (Block Address) mod (Number of Lines)

g.data-narration: In fully associative cache, any memory block can be placed in ANY cache line, offering maximum flexibility.
g.text: Any memory block can be placed in ANY cache line - maximum flexibility.
Block can go to ANY line!
Pro: Best hit rate | Con: Expensive

g.data-narration: Set-associative cache divides cache into sets with multiple ways, balancing flexibility and hardware cost.
g.text: Combines benefits: cache divided into sets, each with multiple ways.
Set Index = (Block Address) mod (Number of Sets)

g.data-narration: A cache line contains data, tag address bits, valid bit, and optionally a dirty bit for write-back policies.
Cache exploits temporal locality (reusing recent items) and spatial locality (accessing nearby items) for effectiveness.
g.text: Recently accessed items will be accessed again
Items near accessed items will be accessed

g.data-narration: Write-through updates both cache and memory on every write; write-back delays memory update until eviction.
Three cache miss types: compulsory (first access), capacity (cache too small), and conflict (mapping collisions).
When cache is full, replacement policies decide which block to evict: LRU, FIFO, or Random selection.
Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) formula combines hit time and miss penalty based on hit rate.
g.text: AMAT = Hit Time + (Miss Rate × Miss Penalty)

g.data-narration: Solve direct-mapped: 64 lines, 16 bytes/line. Find cache index for address 0x1234.
Set-associative: 4-way cache, 256KB total, 64-byte lines. Find number of sets.
Key formulas: cache size calculation, AMAT, address decomposition, and mapping formulas.
Exam tips: memorize mapping techniques, know cache size formula, master address decomposition, and remember AMAT.
g.text: 1. Memorize three mapping techniques and trade-offs
2. Remember: Cache Size = Lines × Line Size
3. Master address decomposition - tag, index, offset

g.data-narration: Avoid mistakes: don't confuse lines with sets, remember offset bits don't join tag comparison.
g.text: ✗ Confusing cache lines with sets - a line is one location
✗ Offset bits don't participate in tag comparison

g.data-narration: Effective revision: practice address decomposition, draw mapping diagrams, review AMAT calculations.
g.text: 1. Practice address decomposition - identify tag, index, offset bits
2. Draw diagrams for all three mapping techniques

g.data-narration: In summary: cache memory uses mapping techniques to store frequently accessed data, exploiting locality for improved performance.
g.text: Summary: Cache optimizes memory access through mapping and locality
